flagged.code:
import * as acm from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-certificatemanager';

new acm.Certificate(this, 'Cert', { domainName: 'example.com' });
flagged.explanation: validation is omitted, so CloudFormation defaults ValidationMethod to EMAIL. The check flags any certificate whose validation method is not DNS, because email validation requires manual renewal.

fixed.explanation: validation: CertificateValidation.fromDns() sets ValidationMethod to DNS, which supports automated renewal, so the finding clears.

flagged.explanation: The GET method uses authorizationType NONE, leaving the backing Lambda invokable by anyone on the internet.

fixed.explanation: A Cognito user pool authorizer is attached so the method requires a valid bearer token before invoking the Lambda.

flagged.explanation: A default RestApi uses an EDGE (non-PRIVATE) endpoint type, so it is reachable from the public internet. The check flags any REST API whose endpoint type is not PRIVATE.

fixed.explanation: Setting the endpoint type to EndpointType.PRIVATE restricts the API to a VPC endpoint, so the check passes.

flagged.explanation: authenticationType is API_KEY. The check flags AppSync GraphQL APIs whose primary authentication is an API key, which is only appropriate for development or public read-only access.

fixed.explanation: Switching authenticationType to AWS_IAM (Cognito User Pools or OIDC also work) removes the API-key authentication finding.

flagged.explanation: The API has no LogConfig and XrayEnabled is not true. The check flags AppSync APIs that lack request/response logging and X-Ray tracing.

fixed.explanation: Adding logConfig with fieldLogLevel: ERROR and setting xrayEnabled: true satisfies both the logging and tracing conditions.

flagged.explanation: AssociatePublicIpAddress is true, so every instance launched from this configuration receives a public IP and is reachable directly from the internet.

fixed.explanation: Instances launch into private subnets with no public IP, reaching the internet only via NAT and fronted by a load balancer if needed.

flagged.explanation: The ASG is attached to a load balancer target group but its HealthCheckType defaults to EC2. EC2 health checks only catch instance-level failures and miss application-level outages the load balancer would otherwise detect.

fixed.explanation: Setting HealthCheckType to ELB lets the load balancer's health checks drive instance replacement, so unhealthy-but-running instances are recycled. The grace period gives new instances time to bootstrap before probing begins.

flagged.explanation: The backup rule has neither CopyActions (no cross-region copy for disaster recovery) nor a Lifecycle (no retention / cold-storage transition). CDK Insights flags AWS::Backup::BackupPlan rules missing cross-region copy or lifecycle configuration.

fixed.explanation: The rule now defines a lifecycle (moveToColdStorageAfterDays + deleteAfterDays) and a cross-region copyActions entry to a DR vault. With both present the finding clears.

flagged.explanation: The backup vault has neither a customer-managed KMS key (EncryptionKeyArn) nor any vault lock, so cdk-insights raises the two backup-vault-hardening findings (default AWS-managed key + no vault lock).

fixed.explanation: Supplying encryptionKeyArn plus an inline lockConfiguration (minRetentionDays) satisfies both branches of the check — the vault is CMK-encrypted and lock-protected — so zero backup-vault-hardening findings are emitted.

flagged.explanation: ALLOW_ALL lets viewers connect over plain HTTP, exposing requests and responses to interception.

fixed.explanation: REDIRECT_TO_HTTPS forces all viewer traffic onto encrypted HTTPS connections.

flagged.explanation: The distribution defines no Logging block, so DistributionConfig.Logging.Bucket is absent. The check flags any CloudFront distribution that has no access logging configured.

fixed.explanation: enableLogging: true makes CDK provision a logs bucket and populate DistributionConfig.Logging.Bucket, so the distribution now has access logging and the finding clears.

flagged.explanation: A custom ACM viewer certificate is attached with minimumProtocolVersion set to TLSv1, which is on the check's outdated list (SSLv3, TLSv1, TLSv1_2016, TLSv1.1_2016). A custom certificate with the version omitted also flags, because it falls back to the old TLSv1 CloudFront default.

fixed.explanation: TLSv1.2_2021 (SecurityPolicyProtocol.TLS_V1_2_2021) is a modern policy that is not on the outdated list, so the check passes.

flagged.explanation: No webAclId is set on the distribution, so DistributionConfig.WebACLId is absent. The check flags distributions that have no AWS WAF WebACL associated.

fixed.explanation: Associating a WAF WebACL ARN via webAclId populates DistributionConfig.WebACLId, so the distribution is protected by WAF and the finding clears.

flagged.explanation: The stack provisions data infrastructure but defines no CloudTrail trail, so API activity is never recorded.

fixed.explanation: A multi-region CloudTrail trail with log-file validation captures account-wide API activity for audit.

flagged.explanation: The Alarm is created with no AlarmActions and no TreatMissingData, so no one is notified when it enters ALARM and its behaviour on missing data points is undefined. CDK Insights flags AWS::CloudWatch::Alarm resources lacking alarm actions or missing-data handling.

fixed.explanation: An SNS topic is wired up via addAlarmAction so state changes trigger a notification, and treatMissingData is set explicitly. With both AlarmActions and TreatMissingData present the finding clears.

flagged.explanation: RetentionDays.INFINITE leaves the log group with no RetentionInDays, so logs are retained indefinitely and storage cost grows forever. CDK Insights flags AWS::Logs::LogGroup resources with no explicit retention period.

fixed.explanation: Setting retention to RetentionDays.ONE_MONTH emits an explicit RetentionInDays, bounding storage cost. The finding clears once a retention period is set.

flagged.explanation: The log group has no KmsKeyId, so log data at rest is encrypted only with the default service key rather than a customer-managed KMS key. CDK Insights flags AWS::Logs::LogGroup resources without a customer-managed key.

fixed.explanation: A customer-managed KMS key is passed via encryptionKey, emitting KmsKeyId on the log group. The finding clears once the log group is encrypted with a customer-managed key.

flagged.explanation: The nested Policies.PasswordPolicy has MinimumLength 8 (<12) and RequireSymbols false, so the check emits two cognito-password-policy-weak findings. MFA is ON so the MFA rule does not also fire.

fixed.explanation: MinimumLength is 12 and every RequireX flag is true, so the password-policy check emits nothing. MFA stays ON so the MFA rule is also silent.

flagged.explanation: MfaConfiguration is OFF, so the MFA check fires. A strong nested Policies.PasswordPolicy keeps the separate password-policy rule quiet, isolating the MFA finding.

fixed.explanation: MfaConfiguration is ON (with an enabled MFA method), so the MFA check no longer fires; the strong password policy keeps the password rule quiet too.

flagged.explanation: The user pool defines no UserPoolAddOns, so AdvancedSecurityMode is absent. The check flags user pools without advanced security (compromised-credential detection and adaptive authentication).

fixed.explanation: advancedSecurityMode: AdvancedSecurityMode.ENFORCED emits UserPoolAddOns.AdvancedSecurityMode=ENFORCED, clearing the finding.

flagged.explanation: The Lambda references the VPC-only RDS endpoint (via an environment variable / Fn::GetAtt dependency) but has no VpcConfig, so it can never reach the database — the cross-resource check fires.

fixed.explanation: The Lambda now runs inside the VPC (vpc + isolated subnets) and its security group is allowed to the database port, so the connectivity mismatch is resolved and no cross-resource finding is emitted.

flagged.explanation: The AppSync GraphQL API has no associated WAFv2 WebACL, leaving it exposed to common web exploits and without request-level access control. This is a cross-resource check: it flags an AWS::AppSync::GraphQLApi with no AWS::WAFv2::WebACLAssociation pointing at its ARN.

fixed.explanation: A CfnWebACL is created and a CfnWebACLAssociation binds it to the API's ARN (api.attrArn). Once a WebACLAssociation references the API the finding clears.

flagged.explanation: The RDS instance owns its master credentials via a hardcoded MasterUserPassword (no ManageMasterUserPassword / MasterUserSecret), so the stack-architecture credential check fires; the absence of alarms adds a second stack-architecture finding.

fixed.explanation: manageMasterUserPassword: true moves credentials to RDS-managed Secrets Manager rotation (the credential check skips it), and a CloudWatch Alarm covers the "no alarms for critical resources" branch — so zero stack-architecture-recommendations findings remain.

flagged.explanation: The table defaults to PROVISIONED billing with no Application Auto Scaling target, so throughput is fixed and cannot absorb spikes.

fixed.explanation: PAY_PER_REQUEST billing lets DynamoDB scale capacity automatically with demand, removing the need for a fixed provisioned target.

flagged.explanation: With no SSESpecification, the table falls back to the AWS-owned key, which you cannot inspect, audit, or rotate.

fixed.explanation: A customer-managed KMS key sets SSESpecification.SSEEnabled with an auditable, rotatable KMSMasterKeyId.

flagged.explanation: The table has no PointInTimeRecoverySpecification, so the PITR check fires. (A dedup fix now keeps this distinct from other DynamoDB cdkInsights findings.)

fixed.explanation: pointInTimeRecoverySpecification.pointInTimeRecoveryEnabled is true and server-side encryption is set, so PITR is enabled and no PITR finding — or encryption co-finding — is produced.

flagged.explanation: No StreamSpecification is set, so item-level changes are not captured for event-driven consumers or replication.

fixed.explanation: Enabling a stream with NEW_AND_OLD_IMAGES publishes every item change for downstream processing.

flagged.explanation: Encrypted is false, so the check flags the volume. (It also defaults to gp2, which the same ruleId flags as a cost item — the conflation.)

fixed.explanation: encrypted: true removes the encryption finding and volumeType gp3 removes the gp2 cost finding, so the ebs-volume-unencrypted ruleId produces nothing.

flagged.explanation: The instance is placed in a public subnet with associatePublicIpAddress true, giving it an internet-routable IP.

fixed.explanation: The instance runs in a private subnet with no public IP, reaching the internet only outbound through a NAT gateway.

flagged.explanation: An ingress rule opens SSH (tcp/22) to 0.0.0.0/0, producing the unrestricted-ingress and dangerous-port findings (both under this ruleId).

fixed.explanation: The group allows a single narrow ingress (private CIDR, HTTPS) and, with allowAllOutbound false, has no 0.0.0.0/0 egress and is not empty — so none of the security-group finding branches fire.

flagged.explanation: The instance is hard-coded to t2.micro. The check flags any AWS::EC2::Instance whose InstanceType begins with the previous-generation t2 family, which delivers lower baseline performance and worse price/performance than current generations.

fixed.explanation: Moving to t3.micro (current generation) resolves the finding: t3 offers a stronger CPU baseline, unlimited burst by default, and better cost efficiency. Any non-t2 current-generation type satisfies the rule.

flagged.explanation: MapPublicIpOnLaunch=true makes any instance launched into this subnet internet-reachable by default, with no explicit opt-in at the ENI level. The check flags subnets that set this flag to true.

fixed.explanation: Setting MapPublicIpOnLaunch=false (the AWS default) clears the finding. Instances that genuinely need a public IP should opt in explicitly at the ENI level rather than inheriting one from the subnet.

flagged.explanation: The check flags a security group named 'default' that carries ingress or egress rules. Default security groups cannot be deleted and should be left empty; adding rules to them is an anti-pattern that widens the blast radius.

fixed.explanation: The default group is left with no rules, and traffic is governed by a dedicated, purpose-built security group instead. The rule only inspects groups named 'default', so the custom group is unaffected.

flagged.explanation: No AWS::EC2::FlowLog targets this VPC, so network traffic is not captured. The check flags any VPC that has no associated flow log, leaving a gap in security analysis and troubleshooting.

fixed.explanation: Adding a FlowLog whose ResourceType is VPC and whose ResourceId references the VPC resolves the finding. Traffic metadata is now delivered to CloudWatch Logs for auditing and diagnostics.

flagged.explanation: A default ec2.Vpc creates managed NAT Gateways for its private subnets. The check flags every AWS::EC2::NatGateway for cost awareness: each incurs an hourly charge plus per-GB data-processing fees.

fixed.explanation: Setting natGateways: 0 removes the managed NAT Gateways entirely, so there are no AWS::EC2::NatGateway resources to flag. Private-subnet access to AWS services is provided through cheaper VPC endpoints.

flagged.explanation: Images pushed to this repository are never scanned for vulnerabilities because scan-on-push is disabled.

fixed.explanation: Enabling scan-on-push triggers a vulnerability scan automatically for every image pushed.

flagged.explanation: The repository has no LifecyclePolicy, so old and untagged images accumulate indefinitely and drive up storage cost. The check flags any ECR repository without a lifecycle policy.

fixed.explanation: Adding a LifecyclePolicy with a LifecyclePolicyText clears the finding. Untagged images are now expired automatically after 14 days, keeping storage bounded.

flagged.explanation: ImageTagMutability is unset, so it defaults to MUTABLE: an existing tag can be overwritten by a new push. That breaks deployment consistency and lets a supply-chain change slip in under a trusted tag.

fixed.explanation: Setting ImageTagMutability to IMMUTABLE prevents any tag from being overwritten once pushed, guaranteeing that a given tag always resolves to the same image.

flagged.explanation: The database password is injected as a plaintext environment variable, exposing it in the task definition.

fixed.explanation: Using the secrets property with ecs.Secret.fromSecretsManager resolves the password from Secrets Manager at runtime instead of storing it in plaintext.

flagged.explanation: The service uses the default rolling-update (ECS) controller but does not enable the deployment circuit breaker, so a deployment that never reaches a steady state is left running on failing tasks instead of being stopped and rolled back.

fixed.explanation: Enabling the deployment circuit breaker with Rollback: true means a failed rolling deployment is automatically stopped and reverted to the last healthy task set, instead of leaving the service degraded.

flagged.explanation: The container definition has no LogConfiguration, so its stdout/stderr is not shipped anywhere. The check flags each container that lacks a log driver, leaving no way to monitor or debug the workload.

fixed.explanation: Adding a LogConfiguration with the awslogs driver routes container logs to a CloudWatch log group, clearing the finding for every container in the task definition.

flagged.explanation: Service Connect is enabled but ServiceConnectConfiguration has no LogConfiguration, so inter-service request metadata (URL, status, latency) is never captured, leaving that traffic unaudited.

fixed.explanation: Adding a LogConfiguration with a log driver to the ServiceConnectConfiguration captures the proxy's traffic metadata in CloudWatch for audit, debugging, and incident response.

flagged.explanation: Neither task-level Cpu/Memory nor any container-level limit is set, so a runaway container has no ceiling and scheduling is unpredictable. The check flags a task definition when it has no task-level sizing and a container carries no CPU/memory limit.

fixed.explanation: Setting Cpu and Memory at the task level gives the task a hard resource envelope, which the check accepts as configured sizing even when individual containers omit their own limits.

flagged.explanation: The image reference ends in :latest, a mutable tag. Two deployments of the same task definition can pull different code, so releases are not reproducible and a changed upstream image can land with no code change.

fixed.explanation: Referencing an immutable version tag (or a @sha256 digest) makes every deployment pull the exact image you tested. The check treats any non-latest, non-empty tag as pinned.

flagged.explanation: The EFS file system is created with encrypted: false, leaving stored data unencrypted at rest.

fixed.explanation: Setting encrypted: true with a customer-managed KMS key enables at-rest encryption on the EFS file system at creation time.

flagged.explanation: The cluster API endpoint is public with PublicAccessCidrs of 0.0.0.0/0 and no private access, exposing the Kubernetes API to the entire internet.

fixed.explanation: Enabling private access and restricting public CIDRs to a known admin range keeps node traffic in-VPC and limits API exposure.

flagged.explanation: No EncryptionConfig for "secrets" means Kubernetes secrets are stored unencrypted in etcd.

fixed.explanation: Adding EncryptionConfig with a KMS key enables envelope encryption of secrets at rest in etcd.

flagged.explanation: No Logging.ClusterLogging.EnabledTypes are declared, so none of the control plane log streams reach CloudWatch. The check flags any EKS cluster with zero control plane log types enabled.

fixed.explanation: Enabling all five recommended control plane log types (api, audit, authenticator, controllerManager, scheduler) sends full audit and diagnostic logs to CloudWatch and clears both the missing-types and no-logging findings.

flagged.explanation: The replication group disables both at-rest and in-transit encryption, leaving cached data and traffic unprotected.

fixed.explanation: Enabling atRestEncryptionEnabled and transitEncryptionEnabled encrypts the cache data both at rest and in transit.

flagged.explanation: Transit encryption is on but no AuthToken is set, so any client that reaches the endpoint can issue Redis commands without authenticating.

fixed.explanation: Adding an AuthToken requires clients to authenticate before running Redis commands over the encrypted connection.

flagged.explanation: The group has a replica but AutomaticFailoverEnabled is left off, so a primary failure will not promote the replica automatically.

fixed.explanation: Enabling both automatic failover and Multi-AZ promotes a replica across AZs when the primary fails.

flagged.explanation: The load balancer only serves plaintext HTTP on port 80, so client traffic is never encrypted in transit.

fixed.explanation: A TLS-terminating HTTPS:443 listener with an ACM certificate is added and HTTP:80 redirects to it, encrypting all client traffic.

flagged.explanation: The ALB has no deletion protection, so it can be deleted accidentally (e.g. a stray CloudFormation change).

fixed.explanation: deletionProtection: true sets the deletion_protection.enabled attribute so the load balancer cannot be deleted until it is turned off.

flagged.explanation: The ALB has no access_logs attribute, so request access logs are not captured for auditing or troubleshooting.

fixed.explanation: Enabling access_logs.s3.enabled with a target bucket makes the ALB write request access logs to S3.

flagged.explanation: The HTTPS listener omits SslPolicy, so it falls back to ELB's default ELBSecurityPolicy-2016-08, which still permits TLS 1.0 and is on the check's outdated list. An omitted SSL policy on an HTTPS/TLS listener now flags for this reason.

fixed.explanation: sslPolicy: SslPolicy.RECOMMENDED_TLS (ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06) is a modern policy not on the outdated list, so the finding clears.

flagged.explanation: The Allow statement grants events:PutEvents to a wildcard principal with no condition, so any AWS account can publish to the bus.

fixed.explanation: Adding an aws:PrincipalOrgID condition restricts publishing to accounts within your AWS Organization instead of the whole internet.

flagged.explanation: A rule created with no `targets` synthesizes an AWS::Events::Rule with no `Targets`, so every matched event is silently dropped — no action is ever taken. The check flags a rule that has no targets (HIGH, Reliability), and separately flags a rule whose `State` is DISABLED (MEDIUM).

fixed.explanation: Adding at least one target (here an SNS topic) makes the synthesized rule carry a non-empty `Targets` array, so matched events are actually delivered. Because the rule is also left ENABLED (the default), neither the no-targets nor the disabled branch of the check fires.

flagged.explanation: The JDBC connection has no PhysicalConnectionRequirements (no VPC subnet placement) and its URL does not enforce SSL, so traffic is neither network-isolated nor guaranteed encrypted in transit. CDK Insights flags AWS::Glue::Connection JDBC connections lacking VPC placement or SSL enforcement.

fixed.explanation: physicalConnectionRequirements now pins the connection to a subnet and security group, the URL carries useSSL=true, and JDBC_ENFORCE_SSL is set. With subnet placement and SSL enforcement present the finding clears.

flagged.explanation: The Glue job has no SecurityConfiguration attached, so job bookmarks, S3 targets and CloudWatch logs are not encrypted with KMS. CDK Insights flags AWS::Glue::Job resources without a SecurityConfiguration.

fixed.explanation: A CfnSecurityConfiguration enabling SSE-KMS / CSE-KMS across S3, CloudWatch and job bookmarks is created and referenced via the job's securityConfiguration property. The finding clears once a SecurityConfiguration is attached.

flagged.explanation: Granting actions '*' on resources '*' gives the role unrestricted access to every API on every resource in the account.

fixed.explanation: Scoping the statement to specific S3 actions on a single bucket ARN follows least privilege and no longer trips the wildcard check.

flagged.explanation: The role's trust policy allows another AWS account (111122223333) to assume it. Cross-account trust widens your security boundary, so it must be deliberate and scoped.

fixed.explanation: The role is now assumed by an AWS service principal within your own account instead of an external account, so there is no cross-account trust to review. If cross-account access is genuinely required, keep the external principal but add a Condition (e.g. sts:ExternalId) to lock it down.

flagged.explanation: The managed policy is attached directly to the IAM user. CIS AWS Foundations 1.15 requires users to inherit permissions through groups so access can be managed centrally.

fixed.explanation: The policy is attached to a group, and the user inherits permissions by group membership. The user itself carries no inline or managed policies, satisfying CIS 1.15.

flagged.explanation: This role has no permissions boundary. Without one, nothing caps the maximum permissions the role (or anything it creates) can be granted, leaving room for privilege escalation.

fixed.explanation: A permissions boundary is attached, so the role's effective permissions can never exceed the intersection of its policies and the boundary. Use a boundary tailored to your organisation rather than PowerUserAccess in production.

flagged.explanation: Setting encryption to UNENCRYPTED disables server-side encryption on the Kinesis data stream.

fixed.explanation: Using StreamEncryption.KMS with a KMS key enables server-side encryption for records in the stream.

flagged.explanation: The stream keeps the default 24-hour retention, leaving no room to replay or reprocess records after a longer outage.

fixed.explanation: Extending retention to 72 hours provides a multi-day window to replay records during recovery.

flagged.explanation: A Deny on kms:* for Principal "*" with no root carveout locks every identity — including the account root — out of the key, making it effectively unrecoverable.

fixed.explanation: The Deny uses NotPrincipal to exclude the account root, so it can never lock the account out of its own key, while still denying every other principal.

flagged.explanation: The key policy grants an Allow to a wildcard principal (AnyPrincipal renders Principal.AWS = '*') with no scoping Condition, so any AWS account or anonymous caller can use the key. This is a critical exposure of key material.

fixed.explanation: Access is granted to a specific IAM role rather than a wildcard principal, so the key policy has no public statement. (Key rotation is also enabled as a best practice.)

flagged.explanation: fn.addPermission with an AnyPrincipal (Principal "*") and no source restriction grants invoke to any AWS account — the check flags it CRITICAL public.

fixed.explanation: The permission is scoped to the S3 service principal with both a SourceArn and SourceAccount, so it is neither a wildcard nor unrestricted — no finding.

flagged.explanation: A secret value is stored in a plaintext environment variable (API_KEY), exposing it to anyone with read access to the function config.

fixed.explanation: The secret lives in Secrets Manager and only its ARN is passed in, so no sensitive value sits in the environment.

flagged.explanation: nodejs18.x is past end-of-life and no longer receives security patches.

fixed.explanation: nodejs22.x is a currently supported runtime that still receives security updates.

flagged.explanation: `memorySize: 2048` sets `MemorySize` above the 1024 MB threshold the check watches. Over-provisioned memory is billed on every invocation, so the rule warns about the cost (MEDIUM, Cost Optimization).

fixed.explanation: A `memorySize` of 512 MB is at or below the 1024 MB threshold, so the high-memory warning does not fire. Right-size the value against observed usage rather than defaulting high.

flagged.explanation: Runtime.NODEJS_LATEST is a variable runtime; the cdk-insights aspect (wired via Aspects.of(app)) records the __usesVariableRuntime flag and the check flags it. Requires the aspect — a bare synth cannot detect it.

fixed.explanation: Pinning Runtime.NODEJS_22_X means the aspect records no variable-runtime flag, so the check emits nothing.

flagged.explanation: A default `ec2.Vpc` provisions a NAT Gateway, and attaching the function with `vpc` gives it a `VpcConfig.SubnetIds`. The check fires only when the template has both a NAT Gateway and a VPC-attached Lambda, warning about accumulating NAT charges (MEDIUM, Cost Optimization).

fixed.explanation: Configuring the VPC with `natGateways: 0` and isolated subnets (reaching AWS services via VPC endpoints instead) removes the NAT Gateway from the template. With no NAT Gateway the check short-circuits and never warns.

flagged.explanation: A function with no `deadLetterQueue` synthesizes without `DeadLetterConfig.TargetArn`, so failed asynchronous invocations are dropped with no capture path. The check flags the missing DLQ (Reliability); severity depends on the detected invocation mode.

fixed.explanation: Passing `deadLetterQueue` sets `DeadLetterConfig.TargetArn`, giving failed async invocations a durable sink, so the rule does not fire. (An `EventInvokeConfig` `DestinationConfig.OnFailure` destination is the modern equivalent and is also accepted.)

flagged.explanation: With `reservedConcurrentExecutions` unset, `ReservedConcurrentExecutions` is absent from the template, so the function can consume all of the account's shared concurrency and starve other functions. The check flags the missing setting (LOW, Reliability).

fixed.explanation: Setting `reservedConcurrentExecutions` caps the function's concurrency and reserves capacity for it, controlling its scaling behavior and protecting the rest of the account. With the value present the rule does not fire.

flagged.explanation: Client authentication permits unauthenticated access, so any client able to reach the brokers can produce and consume without credentials.

fixed.explanation: Unauthenticated access is disabled and SASL/IAM authentication is required, so only IAM-authorized clients can connect.

flagged.explanation: Client-broker traffic is set to PLAINTEXT and inter-broker encryption is disabled, so Kafka data travels unencrypted.

fixed.explanation: Setting clientBroker to TLS, inCluster to true, and a customer-managed dataVolumeKmsKeyId encrypts traffic in transit and broker volumes at rest.

flagged.explanation: No LoggingInfo.BrokerLogs destination is enabled, so Kafka broker logs are never shipped to CloudWatch, Firehose, or S3.

fixed.explanation: Enabling CloudWatch broker logs ships Kafka broker logs for monitoring and troubleshooting.

flagged.explanation: Both at-rest encryption and node-to-node encryption are disabled, leaving indexed data and inter-node traffic unprotected.

fixed.explanation: Enabling encryptionAtRest and nodeToNodeEncryption encrypts stored data and traffic between cluster nodes.

flagged.explanation: The domain has neither AdvancedSecurityOptions (fine-grained access control) nor VPCOptions, so it lacks document/field-level security and network isolation. CDK Insights flags AWS::OpenSearchService::Domain resources without fine-grained access control or VPC placement.

fixed.explanation: The domain now enables advancedSecurityOptions with an IAM master user and is placed inside a VPC via vpcOptions (with encryption at rest, node-to-node encryption and enforced HTTPS, which FGAC requires). With both FGAC and VPC placement present the finding clears.

flagged.explanation: The domain (already hardened with FGAC, VPC and encryption) publishes no logs: LogPublishingOptions omits AUDIT_LOGS and ES_APPLICATION_LOGS, so there is no audit trail or application-error visibility in CloudWatch. CDK Insights flags AWS::OpenSearchService::Domain resources without audit or application logs.

fixed.explanation: logPublishingOptions now enables both AUDIT_LOGS and ES_APPLICATION_LOGS to a CloudWatch log group. The finding clears once audit and application logging are published.

flagged.explanation: The RDS instance sets storageEncrypted: false, so the underlying storage is not encrypted at rest.

fixed.explanation: Setting storageEncrypted: true with a customer-managed KMS key encrypts the RDS instance storage at rest.

flagged.explanation: publiclyAccessible true with a public subnet gives the database a routable endpoint reachable from the internet.

fixed.explanation: The instance sits in private subnets with publiclyAccessible false, so it is reachable only from inside the VPC.

flagged.explanation: A standalone PostgreSQL instance sets BackupRetentionPeriod to 3 days — below the 7-day minimum, limiting the point-in-time-recovery window.

fixed.explanation: Raising BackupRetentionPeriod to 7 days gives a full week of automated backups for point-in-time recovery.

flagged.explanation: A standalone PostgreSQL instance is created without DeletionProtection, so the database can be destroyed by an accidental stack update or delete.

fixed.explanation: Setting DeletionProtection: true blocks accidental deletion until the flag is explicitly removed.

flagged.explanation: No MonitoringInterval is set, so enhanced monitoring is off and OS-level metrics are never published to CloudWatch.

fixed.explanation: A MonitoringInterval of 60 seconds plus a monitoring role turns on enhanced monitoring for OS-level metrics.

flagged.explanation: EnableCloudwatchLogsExports is not configured, so engine logs (error, slow query) are never exported to CloudWatch.

fixed.explanation: Exporting the postgresql and upgrade log types ships engine logs to CloudWatch for retention and analysis.

flagged.explanation: The managed master-user secret uses the AWS-managed aws/secretsmanager key, which cannot be audited or rotated on your schedule.

fixed.explanation: Pinning MasterUserSecret.KmsKeyId to a customer-managed KMS key puts the master secret under your own key policy and rotation.

flagged.explanation: A Multi-AZ instance still uses legacy gp2 storage, which costs more and offers lower baseline performance than gp3.

fixed.explanation: Switching StorageType to gp3 lowers cost and raises baseline throughput for the Multi-AZ instance.

flagged.explanation: publiclyAccessible true exposes the Redshift cluster endpoint to the public internet.

fixed.explanation: The cluster is placed in a private subnet group with publiclyAccessible false, keeping its endpoint inside the VPC.

flagged.explanation: The Redshift cluster sets encrypted: false, so data at rest in the cluster is not encrypted.

fixed.explanation: Setting encrypted: true with a KmsKeyId enables at-rest encryption for the Redshift cluster using a customer-managed key.

flagged.explanation: No LoggingProperties are configured, so connection, user, and query-activity logs are never delivered to S3 or CloudWatch.

fixed.explanation: Configuring LoggingProperties with CloudWatch log exports captures connection and user-activity audit trails.

flagged.code:
import * as route53 from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-route53';

new route53.PublicHostedZone(this, 'Zone', { zoneName: 'example.com' });
flagged.explanation: A PUBLIC hosted zone is declared with no accompanying AWS::Route53::DNSSEC resource. The check flags public hosted zones without DNSSEC signing; private/VPC zones are skipped, so a public zone is required to trigger it.

fixed.explanation: Adding a CfnDNSSEC resource pointing at the zone's hostedZoneId enables DNSSEC signing, clearing the finding.

flagged.explanation: The health check uses type: HTTP, which is plaintext. The check flags HTTP and HTTP_STR_MATCH health checks (and, separately, the 30-second standard request interval).

fixed.explanation: Using type: HTTPS validates the endpoint's certificate, and a 10-second requestInterval avoids the standard-interval sub-check, so no finding is raised.

flagged.explanation: A public hosted zone (no VPCs) with no QueryLoggingConfig triggers the check.

fixed.explanation: The same public zone now carries an inline QueryLoggingConfig.CloudWatchLogsLogGroupArn, which the check reads, so no finding is emitted.

flagged.explanation: A blanket Deny on bucket administration for every principal with no carveout locks the account out of managing or deleting its own bucket.

fixed.explanation: Switching to notPrincipals with the account root exempts the root from the Deny, keeping a recovery path while blocking everyone else.

flagged.explanation: BLOCK_ACLS leaves BlockPublicPolicy and RestrictPublicBuckets false (and the bucket is unencrypted/unversioned), so the s3Buckets check emits several findings all tagged s3-bucket-public-access.

fixed.explanation: A fully hardened bucket — BLOCK_ALL public-access, S3-managed encryption, versioning, and enforced SSL — clears every s3-bucket-public-access branch, yielding zero for that ruleId. (enforceSSL adds a separate bucket-policy resource under a different rule.)

flagged.explanation: The bucket policy grants access without denying non-TLS requests, so the bucket accepts plain HTTP.

fixed.explanation: enforceSSL adds a Deny on aws:SecureTransport=false, rejecting any non-HTTPS request.

flagged.code:
import { Bucket } from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-s3';

new Bucket(this, 'DataBucket');
flagged.explanation: The bucket has no server access logging configured. CIS AWS Foundations 2.1.2 requires access logs, and they are essential for incident forensics.

fixed.explanation: Access logs are delivered to a dedicated (here, pre-existing) logging bucket via serverAccessLogsBucket, so LoggingConfiguration.DestinationBucketName is set. Referencing an existing central log bucket avoids stacking a second unlogged bucket into the same template.

flagged.code:
import { Bucket } from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-s3';

new Bucket(this, 'DataBucket');
flagged.explanation: The bucket has no lifecycle rules, so objects accumulate indefinitely at Standard-storage prices and old versions are never cleaned up.

fixed.explanation: A lifecycle rule transitions objects to Glacier after 90 days and expires them after 365, populating LifecycleConfiguration.Rules and controlling storage cost.

flagged.code:
import { Bucket } from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-s3';

new Bucket(this, 'DataBucket');
flagged.explanation: The bucket does not use S3 Intelligent-Tiering. For data with unpredictable access patterns, this misses automatic cost savings on infrequently accessed objects.

fixed.explanation: An Intelligent-Tiering configuration is defined, so S3 automatically moves objects between access tiers based on usage. A lifecycle transition to the INTELLIGENT_TIERING storage class satisfies the rule equally.

flagged.explanation: This production/critical-looking bucket has no cross-region replication. If the region or bucket is lost, there is no second copy for disaster recovery.

fixed.explanation: The critical bucket now replicates to a second (versioned) bucket via replicationRules, giving a durable copy for disaster recovery. In production, target a bucket in a different region.

flagged.explanation: The secret's resource policy grants an Allow to a wildcard principal with no scoping Condition, making the secret publicly accessible. Anyone could retrieve its value.

fixed.explanation: Read access is granted to a specific IAM role via an identity-based policy, so no public resource policy is attached to the secret. Only that role can retrieve the value.

flagged.explanation: A blanket Deny on topic administration for every principal with no carveout locks the account out of managing or deleting its own topic.

fixed.explanation: Using notPrincipals with the account root exempts the root from the Deny, preserving a recovery path while restricting all other principals.

flagged.explanation: A Topic created with no `masterKey` synthesizes an AWS::SNS::Topic without a `KmsMasterKeyId`, so messages are not encrypted at rest. The check flags any SNS topic missing `KmsMasterKeyId` (HIGH, Security).

fixed.explanation: Passing a `masterKey` sets `KmsMasterKeyId` on the topic, enabling KMS server-side encryption. With the key present the rule does not fire.

flagged.explanation: A blanket Deny on queue administration for every principal with no carveout locks the account out of managing or deleting its own queue.

fixed.explanation: Switching to notPrincipals with the account root exempts the root from the Deny, keeping a recovery path while blocking everyone else.

flagged.explanation: `QueueEncryption.UNENCRYPTED` produces an AWS::SQS::Queue with neither `KmsMasterKeyId` nor `SqsManagedSseEnabled: true`, so message data is stored unencrypted. The check flags a queue only when both encryption modes are absent (HIGH, Security).

fixed.explanation: `QueueEncryption.KMS_MANAGED` sets `KmsMasterKeyId` (alias/aws/sqs) on the queue, satisfying the at-rest encryption requirement. `QueueEncryption.SQS_MANAGED` (which sets `SqsManagedSseEnabled: true`) is equally accepted and also clears the finding.

flagged.explanation: A state machine created without a `logs` option synthesizes with no `LoggingConfiguration`, so execution history is not sent to CloudWatch Logs. The check treats a missing config (or one whose `Level` is absent or OFF) as logging disabled (MEDIUM, Operational Excellence).

fixed.explanation: Supplying `logs` with a destination log group and `LogLevel.ALL` emits a `LoggingConfiguration` with `Level: ALL`, which counts as logging enabled, so the rule does not fire.

flagged.explanation: DefaultAction Allow, no rules, and CloudWatchMetricsEnabled false trigger all three misconfiguration branches (all tagged waf-webacl-misconfigured).

fixed.explanation: DefaultAction Block, at least one rule, and a VisibilityConfig with CloudWatchMetricsEnabled + SampledRequestsEnabled + MetricName clear every branch — zero findings.

flagged.explanation: The WebACL (well-formed here: Block default action, CloudWatch metrics on, one managed rule) has no companion AWS::WAFv2::LoggingConfiguration referencing it. The check flags WebACLs that have no logging configuration.

fixed.explanation: Adding a CfnLoggingConfiguration whose resourceArn references the WebACL (via acl.attrArn) directs analysed requests to a log destination, clearing the finding.

flagged.explanation: The Function URL uses AuthType NONE, so anyone with the URL can invoke the function without authentication.

fixed.explanation: AuthType AWS_IAM requires callers to sign requests with SigV4, so only authorized IAM principals can invoke the URL.

flagged.explanation: The launch template does not set MetadataOptions, so instances default to allowing IMDSv1 — a known SSRF-to-credential-theft path.

fixed.explanation: Setting httpTokens to "required" enforces IMDSv2 session tokens, blocking the simple SSRF requests that IMDSv1 answers.

flagged.code:
import * as s3 from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-s3';

new s3.Bucket(this, 'Bucket');
flagged.explanation: The bucket has no versioning, so an overwritten or deleted object cannot be recovered.

fixed.code:
import * as s3 from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-s3';

new s3.Bucket(this, 'Bucket', { versioned: true });
fixed.explanation: versioned: true keeps prior versions of every object, allowing recovery from accidental deletes/overwrites and ransomware.

flagged.explanation: The table has no deletion protection, so an accidental stack update or delete can destroy it and its data.

fixed.explanation: deletionProtectionEnabled: true blocks deletion of the table until the flag is explicitly removed.

flagged.explanation: The function has no active X-Ray tracing, so distributed traces for latency and error analysis are not captured.

fixed.explanation: tracing: Tracing.ACTIVE sets TracingConfig.Mode to Active so the function emits X-Ray segments.

flagged.explanation: The container runs privileged, giving it host-level access so a container compromise becomes a host compromise.

fixed.explanation: With privileged: false the container is confined to its namespace; grant only specific capabilities via linuxParameters if needed.

flagged.code:
import * as sqs from 'aws-cdk-lib/aws-sqs';

new sqs.CfnQueue(this, 'Queue', {});
flagged.explanation: The queue has no RedrivePolicy, so messages that repeatedly fail processing are dropped instead of captured.

fixed.explanation: A RedrivePolicy routes messages that exceed maxReceiveCount to a dead-letter queue for inspection instead of losing them.

flagged.explanation: A custom domain is configured but the REST API still exposes the default execute-api endpoint, which bypasses the domain and its edge controls.

fixed.explanation: disableExecuteApiEndpoint: true forces all traffic through the custom domain (and any WAF/edge controls) instead of the default URL.

flagged.explanation: The stage sets no method throttling, so a traffic spike can overwhelm backends and run up cost.

fixed.explanation: Method settings with throttlingRateLimit and throttlingBurstLimit cap request rates, protecting integrations and controlling cost.
